bytes.topic: Routing — defining routes, route parameters, grouping and prefixing
bytes.title: Rails Routing — Resources, Parameters, and Namespace Grouping
bytes.body:
Rails routes live in `config/routes.rb` and are matched top-to-bottom. `resources :articles` generates 7 RESTful routes automatically — index, show, new, create, edit, update, destroy — each mapping to a conventional controller action. Use `only:` or `except:` to trim unused routes and keep `rails routes` output clean.

Route parameters are captured as symbols: `get '/articles/:id', to: 'articles#show'` makes `params[:id]` available in the controller. Constraints scope them further: `constraints(id: /\d+/)` rejects non-numeric ids at the router level, before the controller runs.

Grouping routes under a shared path prefix uses `scope '/api'`, but `namespace :api` goes further — it also expects an `Api::` module prefix on the controller class. Use `namespace` for versioned APIs (`namespace :v1`) and `scope` when you want the URL prefix without the module nesting. Nested `resources` model ownership relationships: `resources :articles do; resources :comments; end` produces `/articles/:article_id/comments`, with both ids available in `params`.
bytes.code_snippet:
# config/routes.rb
Rails.application.routes.draw do
  namespace :api do
    namespace :v1 do
      resources :articles, only: [:index, :show, :create] do
        resources :comments, only: [:index, :create]
      end

      get '/search/:query', to: 'search#results',
          constraints: { query: /[^.\/]+/ }
    end
  end

  root 'home#index'
end

bytes.topic: Project Structure — MVC vs vertical slice, feature folders, layering conventions
bytes.title: Rails Project Structure — MVC Default vs. Vertical Slice with Feature Folders
bytes.body:
Rails' default MVC layout (`app/models`, `app/controllers`, `app/services`) collapses under complexity — a 200-model app forces you to hunt across directories for a single feature. Vertical slice architecture fixes this by colocating everything a feature needs under one folder.

Drop a `app/features/` (or `app/domains/`) directory and namespace your classes accordingly. Each slice owns its controller, service object, form object, query object, and serializer. Add the path in `config/application.rb` via `config.autoload_paths << Rails.root.join('app/features')` and Zeitwerk handles the rest — no manual `require` needed.

Slices map cleanly to Rails conventions: controllers still inherit from `ApplicationController`, models stay in `app/models` (they're shared infrastructure), and routes remain in `config/routes.rb` using `namespace :billing` or `scope module: :billing`. This isn't a framework replacement — it's a layering convention on top of Rails. Start vertical slicing when a domain exceeds ~5 controllers or when cross-feature entanglement makes tests brittle.
bytes.code_snippet:
# config/application.rb
config.autoload_paths << Rails.root.join('app/features')

# app/features/billing/invoices_controller.rb
module Billing
  class InvoicesController < ApplicationController
    def create
      result = Billing::CreateInvoice.call(invoice_params)
      render json: result, status: :created
    end
  end
end

# config/routes.rb
namespace :billing do
  resources :invoices, only: [:create, :index]
end

bytes.topic: ORM & Database — query builders vs full ORMs, N+1 prevention, migrations
bytes.title: Rails ActiveRecord — Crushing N+1s and Knowing When to Skip the ORM
bytes.body:
Rails' ActiveRecord is a full ORM: schema, associations, lifecycle callbacks, and SQL generation in one layer. Every `where`, `joins`, and `order` call assembles an Arel AST compiled to SQL at execution time — which means query composition is lazy and chainable until you enumerate.

The classic N+1: `Post.all.each { |p| p.author.name }` fires one query per post. `includes(:author)` resolves it with one of two strategies: `preload` (two separate queries) or `eager_load` (a LEFT OUTER JOIN). Rails picks automatically, but force `eager_load` when you need to filter on the association: `Post.eager_load(:author).where(authors: { verified: true })`. Using `includes` with a `where` on the association silently upgrades to `eager_load`.

When you don't need AR objects at all, reach for `pluck` or `select` with raw SQL — zero instantiation overhead. `Post.joins(:author).pluck('posts.id, authors.name')` returns plain arrays.

Migrations are sequential, versioned schema mutations. Always add indexes at migration time — never retroactively — and use compound indexes for multi-column predicates: `add_index :posts, [:author_id, :published_at]`.
bytes.code_snippet:
# N+1 — fires 1 + N queries
Post.all.each { |p| puts p.author.name }

# Fix 1: preload — 2 queries, no JOIN
Post.includes(:author).each { |p| puts p.author.name }

# Fix 2: eager_load — 1 LEFT OUTER JOIN, filterable on association
Post.eager_load(:author)
    .where(authors: { verified: true })
    .each { |p| puts p.author.name }

# Fix 3: pluck — skip ActiveRecord instantiation entirely
Post.joins(:author).pluck('posts.id, authors.name')

# Migration: compound index at creation time
add_index :posts, [:author_id, :published_at]

bytes.topic: Input Validation — schema validation at the boundary, sanitization, error responses
bytes.title: Rails Input Validation — Schema Validation at the Boundary with Strong Parameters and ActiveModel
bytes.body:
Rails splits validation into two layers: parameter filtering at the controller boundary and model-level schema validation. Strong Parameters (`require`/`permit`) prevent mass-assignment but don't validate shape or types — they silently drop unexpected keys. For tighter boundary validation, reach for `ActiveModel::Attributes` with type coercion on a plain Ruby object (no DB table needed), or use a form object backed by `ActiveModel::Validations`. This catches malformed input before it touches ActiveRecord, keeping your models clean.

For error responses, `render json: { errors: resource.errors.full_messages }, status: :unprocessable_entity` is idiomatic Rails. Pair it with `rescue_from ActionController::ParameterMissing` in `ApplicationController` to return a consistent 400 when a required root key is absent. Avoid leaking internal field names — use `errors.as_json` with `full_messages: true` or a custom serializer. For APIs, the `jsonapi-serializer` gem enforces a spec-compliant error envelope that clients can rely on.
bytes.code_snippet:
# app/forms/create_user_form.rb
class CreateUserForm
  include ActiveModel::Model
  include ActiveModel::Attributes

  attribute :email,    :string
  attribute :age,      :integer
  attribute :role,     :string, default: "viewer"

  validates :email, presence: true, format: { with: URI::MailTo::EMAIL_REGEXP }
  validates :age,   numericality: { greater_than: 0, less_than: 130 }, allow_nil: true
  validates :role,  inclusion: { in: %w[viewer editor admin] }
end

# app/controllers/users_controller.rb
def create
  form = CreateUserForm.new(user_params)
  return render json: { errors: form.errors.full_messages }, status: :unprocessable_entity unless form.valid?

  user = User.create!(email: form.email, age: form.age, role: form.role)
  render json: UserSerializer.new(user), status: :created
end

private

def user_params
  params.require(:user).permit(:email, :age, :role)
end

bytes.topic: Authentication — JWT validation, session handling, auth middleware patterns
bytes.title: Rails JWT Auth — Validation, Decode, and Before-Action Middleware
bytes.body:
Rails has no built-in JWT support, so most apps reach for the `jwt` gem alongside a custom concern. The cleanest pattern: decode and validate in a reusable `Authenticatable` concern, mount it via `before_action :authenticate_user!` in `ApplicationController`, and skip it per-controller with `skip_before_action`.

Decode inside a `rescue` block — `JWT::DecodeError` covers expired tokens, bad signatures, and malformed payloads. Always pass `algorithms: ['HS256']` explicitly; omitting it allows the `none` algorithm attack.

Store the secret in `Rails.application.credentials.jwt_secret` (not an env var read at class load time) so it rotates cleanly per environment. For session-like behavior without cookies, stash the decoded payload in `Current.user` via `ActiveSupport::CurrentAttributes` — this gives you thread-safe, request-scoped access anywhere in the stack without passing the user object through every method call. Avoid storing JWTs in Rails session (`session[]`); that defeats statelessness and inflates cookie size.
bytes.code_snippet:
# app/controllers/concerns/authenticatable.rb
module Authenticatable
  extend ActiveSupport::Concern

  included do
    before_action :authenticate_user!
  end

  private

  def authenticate_user!
    token = request.headers["Authorization"]&.split(" ")&.last
    payload, = JWT.decode(
      token,
      Rails.application.credentials.jwt_secret!,
      true,
      algorithms: ["HS256"]
    )
    Current.user = User.find(payload["sub"])
  rescue JWT::DecodeError
    render json: { error: "Unauthorized" }, status: :unauthorized
  end
end

bytes.topic: Error Handling — global error handlers, consistent error response format
bytes.title: Rails Global Error Handling — Rescue, Render, Repeat
bytes.body:
Rails' `rescue_from` in `ApplicationController` is the idiomatic hook for centralizing error handling across your entire API. Instead of scattering `rescue` blocks through individual controllers, declare handlers once and let Rails dispatch exceptions automatically.

For JSON APIs, pair `rescue_from` with a private `render_error` method that enforces a consistent envelope: `{ error: { code:, message:, details: } }`. This contract lets clients handle errors uniformly without parsing inconsistent shapes.

Map exception classes to HTTP status codes explicitly — don't rely on Rails' default 500 for everything. `ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound` → 404, `ActiveRecord::RecordInvalid` → 422, `ActionController::ParameterMissing` → 400, custom `AuthorizationError` → 403.

For unhandled exceptions, catch `StandardError` as a catch-all last resort and log the full backtrace via `Rails.logger.error` before returning a sanitized 500. Never leak stack traces to clients in production — use `Rails.env.production?` to gate detail exposure.

In Rails 7+, `config.exceptions_app = routes` lets you route exceptions through your own controller for even more control over the response format.

bytes.topic: Testing — unit vs integration, mocking HTTP, test database strategy
bytes.title: Rails Testing — Unit vs Integration, HTTP Mocking, and Database Strategy
bytes.body:
Rails unit tests (model specs) hit the database by default — unlike most frameworks. Use `build_stubbed` from FactoryBot to avoid DB writes entirely when testing pure logic like validations or instance methods. Integration tests (request specs) should hit a real test database; avoid mocking ActiveRecord here since you want the full stack exercised.

For external HTTP calls, use WebMock or VCR. WebMock lets you stub at the net layer — `stub_request(:get, /api.github.com/)` intercepts before any socket opens. VCR records real responses into cassettes, ideal for third-party APIs with complex payloads.

Database strategy: use `database_cleaner-active_record` with `truncation` for system/feature specs and `transaction` for unit/request specs. Transaction rollback is 10–20x faster but breaks specs that spawn threads (Capybara, Sidekiq inline). Set `DatabaseCleaner.strategy = :transaction` globally, override to `:truncation` per spec tag with `js: true`. Keep your test.rb database config pointing to a separate DB — never share with development.

bytes.topic: Security — CORS, rate limiting, security headers, injection prevention
bytes.title: Rails Security Essentials — CORS, Rate Limiting, Headers, and Injection Prevention
bytes.body:
Rails ships with solid defaults, but production apps need deliberate hardening. For CORS, use the `rack-cors` gem: configure `config/initializers/cors.rb` to whitelist specific origins rather than `'*'`. Pair it with `ActionDispatch::SSL` middleware and `config.force_ssl = true` in production.

For rate limiting, `rack-attack` is the Rails standard. Define throttles in an initializer — `Rack::Attack.throttle('api/ip', limit: 100, period: 1.minute)` — and mount it in `config/application.rb` with `config.middleware.use Rack::Attack`.

Security headers are handled by the `secure_headers` gem. Set `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Strict-Transport-Security`, and a tight `Content-Security-Policy` in one initializer block.

For injection prevention, Rails ActiveRecord parameterizes queries automatically — never interpolate user input into `where` strings. Use `sanitize` and `html_escape` (aliased as `h`) in views. For shell calls, avoid `system()` with user input; use `Open3.capture2e` with array args to prevent shell injection.
bytes.code_snippet:
# config/initializers/rack_attack.rb
Rack::Attack.throttle('req/ip', limit: 100, period: 1.minute) do |req|
  req.ip unless req.path.start_with?('/assets')
end

Rack::Attack.throttle('logins/email', limit: 5, period: 20.seconds) do |req|
  req.params['email']&.downcase if req.path == '/users/sign_in' && req.post?
end

Rack::Attack.blocklist('block bad actors') do |req|
  Rack::Attack::Allow2Ban.filter(req.ip, maxretry: 10, findtime: 1.minute, bantime: 1.hour) do
    req.path == '/users/sign_in' && req.post?
  end
end

bytes.topic: Performance — connection pooling, async handlers, response compression
bytes.title: Rails Performance — Connection Pooling, Async Queries, and Response Compression
bytes.body:
Rails' default connection pool is sized to `RAILS_MAX_THREADS` (typically 5), which becomes a silent bottleneck under Puma's multi-threaded concurrency. Set `pool` explicitly in `database.yml` to match your thread count, and tune `checkout_timeout` to surface starvation early rather than letting requests queue silently.

Rails 7.1 introduced `load_async` on ActiveRecord relations, which offloads a query to a background thread from the connection pool. When a controller needs two independent result sets, firing both with `load_async` and materialising them just before render cuts total latency to the duration of the slower query, not the sum of both.

For response compression, drop `Rack::Deflater` into the middleware stack. It transparently gzip-encodes responses when the client sends `Accept-Encoding: gzip`, typically shrinking JSON payloads by 60–80%. Place it above `ActionDispatch::Static` so asset serving is also covered. Avoid double-compression: if Nginx already handles gzip upstream, skip `Rack::Deflater` to prevent wasted CPU on pre-compressed bytes.
bytes.code_snippet:
# config/database.yml
production:
  pool: <%= ENV.fetch("RAILS_MAX_THREADS") { 5 } %>
  checkout_timeout: 5

# config/application.rb
config.middleware.insert_after ActionDispatch::Static, Rack::Deflater

# app/controllers/posts_controller.rb
def index
  posts   = Post.published.order(created_at: :desc).limit(20).load_async
  sidebar = Tag.popular.limit(10).load_async

  render json: { posts: posts.to_a, tags: sidebar.to_a }
end

bytes.topic: API Design — REST conventions, versioning strategy, OpenAPI generation
bytes.title: Rails API Versioning — Namespaces, Constraints, and rswag Docs
bytes.body:
Rails' routing DSL makes API versioning clean with nested namespaces. Use `namespace :v1` to scope both routes and controllers under `Api::V1`, keeping your versions fully isolated. For header-based versioning (Accept: application/vnd.myapp.v2+json), define a custom route constraint class that reads `request.headers['Accept']` and matches on the version string — mount it before the default namespace so it intercepts first.

Inherit from `ActionController::API` instead of `ApplicationController` in your API base class to strip middleware like cookie sessions and view rendering — this alone cuts request overhead measurably.

For OpenAPI generation, `rswag` integrates directly with RSpec: you write request specs with a `swagger_helper`, annotate endpoints using its DSL (`parameter`, `response`, `schema`), then run `rails rswag:specs:swaggerize` to emit a `swagger.json`. This keeps docs co-located with tests and forces them to stay accurate. Pair with `rswag-ui` to mount Swagger UI at `/api-docs` — zero extra infrastructure needed.
bytes.code_snippet:
# config/routes.rb
Rails.application.routes.draw do
  namespace :api do
    namespace :v1 do
      resources :posts, only: [:index, :show, :create]
    end

    namespace :v2 do
      resources :posts, only: [:index, :show, :create]
    end
  end
end

# app/controllers/api/base_controller.rb
module Api
  class BaseController < ActionController::API
    before_action :authenticate_request!
  end
end

# app/controllers/api/v1/posts_controller.rb
module Api
  module V1
    class PostsController < Api::BaseController
      def index
        render json: Post.all, status: :ok
      end
    end
  end
end

bytes.topic: Configuration — env vars, settings classes, secrets management
bytes.title: Rails Configuration — Env Vars, Credentials, and Settings Classes Done Right
bytes.body: Rails gives you three distinct layers for configuration, and mixing them up causes headaches. Use ENV vars for infrastructure concerns (DATABASE_URL, REDIS_URL) that change per deployment. Use Rails credentials (`rails credentials:edit`) for secrets that must be encrypted at rest — API keys, Stripe secrets, JWT signing keys. Access them via `Rails.application.credentials.stripe[:secret_key]`. For structured app-level settings that aren't secrets, use `config/settings.yml` with a gem like `config` (aka Railsconfig), which gives you typed, environment-aware access through `Settings.feature_flags.new_onboarding`. Avoid scattering `ENV.fetch` calls across models and controllers — centralize them in a dedicated `AppConfig` class or an initializer that validates required vars at boot using `ENV.fetch('KEY') { raise 'KEY missing' }`. This turns a runtime crash mid-request into a loud startup failure. In Rails 7.1+, `config.x` namespace lets you attach arbitrary config to the app object (`config.x.max_upload_mb = 10`) without a gem, accessible as `Rails.application.config.x.max_upload_mb` anywhere.
bytes.code_snippet:
# config/initializers/app_config.rb
module AppConfig
  STRIPE_SECRET  = ENV.fetch('STRIPE_SECRET_KEY')  { raise 'STRIPE_SECRET_KEY is not set' }
  REDIS_URL      = ENV.fetch('REDIS_URL', 'redis://localhost:6379/0')
end

# Access encrypted credentials (stored in config/credentials.yml.enc)
stripe_key = Rails.application.credentials.stripe[:secret_key]

# Rails 7.1+ config.x namespace (no gem needed)
# config/application.rb
config.x.uploads.max_size_mb = ENV.fetch('MAX_UPLOAD_MB', '10').to_i

# Anywhere in the app
Rails.application.config.x.uploads.max_size_mb  # => 10

bytes.topic: Serialization — request/response DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, null handling
bytes.title: Rails Serialization — camelCase APIs, snake_case Models, and Null Contracts
bytes.body:
Rails models use snake_case everywhere, but JavaScript frontends expect camelCase. Bridging this manually — renaming keys in controllers or `.as_json` overrides — creates drift fast. The clean solution is Alba (or Blueprinter), which centralizes key transformation and null semantics in a dedicated serializer layer.

With `transform_keys :camel_lower`, Alba rewrites every snake_case attribute automatically. No per-field aliasing needed. For null handling, the critical decision is contract stability: don't use `omit_nil` unless bandwidth is a real constraint — omitting nulls forces clients to guard every key access. Instead, be explicit: always emit `null` so the response shape is predictable and TypeScript types stay clean.

For request DTOs, Rails' Strong Parameters already act as your inbound shape contract. Pair `permit` with explicit type coercions (`params[:age].to_i`) rather than relying on implicit casting. This gives you a serialization layer on both sides: Alba out, Strong Parameters in — no extra gem needed for the inbound path.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Gemfile: gem 'alba'

class UserSerializer
  include Alba::Resource

  transform_keys :camel_lower

  attributes :id, :email, :created_at

  attribute :full_name { |u| "#{u.first_name} #{u.last_name}" }
  attribute :avatar_url { |u| u.avatar.attached? ? Rails.application.routes.url_helpers.url_for(u.avatar) : nil }
  attribute :bio  # emits null if nil — never omit
end

# Controller
render json: UserSerializer.new(@user).serialize
# => {"id":1,"email":"x@y.com","createdAt":"...","fullName":"...","avatarUrl":null,"bio":null}

bytes.topic: Logging — structured logs, correlation IDs, request logging middleware
bytes.title: Rails Structured Logging — Correlation IDs and Request Middleware
bytes.body:
Rails' default logger emits plain-text lines — useless in production when you need to trace a single request across dozens of log entries. Switch to `semantic_logger` or configure `ActiveSupport::Logger` with `broadcast` to emit JSON. The real unlock is correlation IDs: generate a UUID per request in a `before_action` and store it in `Current` (ActiveSupport::CurrentAttributes), then thread it through every log call automatically via a custom log formatter.

Mount a middleware to inject the ID early — before the router — so even 404s and Rack-level errors carry it. Read `X-Request-Id` from the incoming headers first; fall back to `SecureRandom.uuid`. This lets upstream services (load balancers, APMs like Datadog or Honeybadger) correlate distributed traces.

Tag log lines with `Rails.logger.tagged(Current.request_id)` or set `config.log_tags` in `application.rb` to `[:request_id]` — Rails will prepend it to every `ActiveSupport::TaggedLogging` entry automatically. Pair with `lograge` to collapse noisy ActionController multi-line output into a single structured JSON object per request.
bytes.code_snippet:
# config/application.rb
config.log_tags = [:request_id]
config.logger = ActiveSupport::Logger.new(STDOUT)
  .then { ActiveSupport::TaggedLogging.new(_1) }

# app/middleware/correlation_id_middleware.rb
class CorrelationIdMiddleware
  def initialize(app) = @app = app

  def call(env)
    id = env["HTTP_X_REQUEST_ID"] || SecureRandom.uuid
    Current.request_id = id
    env["action_dispatch.request_id"] = id
    @app.call(env)
  end
end

# config/application.rb
config.middleware.insert_before Rails::Rack::Logger, CorrelationIdMiddleware

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — hot reload, code generation, scaffolding CLI
bytes.title: Rails Dev Tooling — Hot Reload, Scaffolding, and CLI Tricks You Should Be Using
bytes.body:
Rails ships with a surprisingly powerful dev tooling surface that most developers underuse. Since Rails 7, `Zeitwerk` handles code reloading automatically — any change to `app/` is picked up without a server restart, governed by `config.enable_reloading` (true by default in development). For views, `ActionController::Live` combined with Turbo Streams gives you real-time UI updates without a full reload cycle.

Scaffolding goes beyond `rails generate scaffold Post title:string body:text`. Use `--no-jbuilder`, `--no-helper`, or `--skip-routes` flags to generate only what you need. Custom generators live in `lib/generators/` and inherit from `Rails::Generators::Base`, letting you codify team conventions into repeatable templates with ERB-based `.tt` files.

The `rails` CLI also exposes `rails notes` (scans TODOs/FIXMEs), `rails routes --grep login` for filtered routing inspection, and `rails runner 'User.find(1).inspect'` for one-off script execution in the app context. Pair `spring` or `bootsnap` for sub-second boot times on large apps.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Generate a scaffold, skipping noise
rails generate scaffold Article title:string:index body:text published:boolean \
  --no-jbuilder --no-helper --no-assets

# Custom generator skeleton
# lib/generators/service/service_generator.rb
class ServiceGenerator < Rails::Generators::NamedBase
  source_root File.expand_path('templates', __dir__)

  def create_service_file
    template 'service.rb.tt', "app/services/#{file_name}_service.rb"
  end
end

# Invoke it
rails generate service UserRegistration

bytes.topic: Background Jobs — task queues, scheduled jobs, worker process patterns
bytes.title: Rails Background Jobs — Sidekiq, ActiveJob, and Worker Process Patterns
bytes.body:
Rails background job processing centers on ActiveJob as the queue adapter abstraction, with Sidekiq as the de facto production backend. Define a job by inheriting from ApplicationJob, set queue_as to control priority lanes, and enqueue with perform_later. Sidekiq reads from Redis-backed queues using a thread pool — configure concurrency in sidekiq.yml alongside named queues and their weights.

For scheduled/recurring jobs, sidekiq-cron or the built-in Sidekiq Pro scheduler lets you define cron expressions in a YAML file loaded at boot. Avoid storing large objects in job arguments — pass an ActiveRecord ID and reload inside perform, since the object state may be stale by execution time.

Retry semantics are critical: Sidekiq retries 25 times with exponential backoff by default. Override sidekiq_options retries: 5 per job class, and implement the sidekiq_retries_exhausted block for dead-job handling. Use discard_on and retry_on at the ActiveJob layer for exception-specific routing. Run workers as separate Puma-adjacent processes — never in-process in production.
bytes.code_snippet:
# app/jobs/send_welcome_email_job.rb
class SendWelcomeEmailJob < ApplicationJob
  queue_as :mailers
  sidekiq_options retries: 3

  discard_on ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound

  sidekiq_retries_exhausted do |msg, ex|
    Sentry.capture_exception(ex, extra: { job_args: msg['args'] })
  end

  def perform(user_id)
    user = User.find(user_id) # reload — never pass the object directly
    UserMailer.welcome(user).deliver_now
  end
end

# Enqueue from anywhere
SendWelcomeEmailJob.perform_later(user.id)
SendWelcomeEmailJob.set(wait: 5.minutes).perform_later(user.id)

bytes.topic: Monitoring — health check endpoints, metrics exposure, distributed tracing
bytes.title: Rails Monitoring — Health Checks, Prometheus Metrics, and OpenTelemetry Tracing
bytes.body: Rails 7.1 ships a built-in `/up` health endpoint via `get "up" => "rails/health#show"` — it returns 200 only when the app boots without error. Extend it by subclassing `Rails::HealthController` and adding custom checks against `ActiveRecord::Base.connection` or `Rails.cache`. For metrics, `prometheus_exporter` is the idiomatic choice: a collector process aggregates stats pushed from Puma workers via `ActiveSupport::Notifications`. Subscribe to `process_action.action_controller` and `sql.active_record` to expose per-endpoint latency and query count gauges without patching middleware. For distributed tracing, `opentelemetry-instrumentation-rails` auto-instruments the full request lifecycle — controllers, Active Record, Active Job, and Action Cable — by registering a Rack middleware and wrapping `ActiveSupport::Notifications`. Configure the OTLP exporter to push spans to Jaeger or Honeycomb. The three signals compose cleanly: health checks for liveness/readiness probes, Prometheus for RED metrics, and OTel traces for root-cause analysis. All three can be wired without monkey-patching core Rails internals.
bytes.code_snippet:
# config/initializers/opentelemetry.rb
OpenTelemetry::SDK.configure do |c|
  c.service_name = "my-rails-app"
  c.use "OpenTelemetry::Instrumentation::Rails"
  c.use "OpenTelemetry::Instrumentation::ActiveRecord"
  c.use "OpenTelemetry::Instrumentation::ActiveJob"
end

# config/routes.rb
get "up" => "rails/health#show"   # liveness probe (Rails 7.1+)

# config/initializers/prometheus.rb
require "prometheus_exporter/instrumentation"
PrometheusExporter::Instrumentation::ActiveRecord.start(
  custom_labels: { app: "my-rails-app" },
  config_labels: [:database, :host]
)

bytes.topic: Framework-specific — Django batteries-included, FastAPI auto-docs, NestJS decorators, Phoenix LiveView
bytes.title: Rails Hotwire — Phoenix LiveView-Style Reactivity Without a JS Framework
bytes.body: Rails ships Hotwire by default — Turbo + Stimulus — giving you Phoenix LiveView-style server-driven UI without React or a separate API layer. Turbo Streams let you push partial HTML updates over ActionCable directly from your models. Declare `broadcasts_to` on an ActiveRecord model and Rails auto-publishes a Turbo Stream on create/update/destroy. On the client, `turbo_stream_from` subscribes and surgically patches the DOM — no full-page reload, no client state management. The broadcast targets a DOM id convention Rails enforces via `dom_id(@post)`, so the server and view stay in sync by convention. Unlike Phoenix LiveView, which diffs a stateful server process, Turbo Streams are stateless partial renders — simpler operationally, no persistent socket process per user. For optimistic UI or finer control, `Turbo::StreamsChannel.broadcast_replace_to` lets you push from anywhere: jobs, service objects, webhooks. Combined with `ActionCable`'s Redis adapter, this scales horizontally with zero front-end build tooling changes. It's the most Rails-idiomatic path to real-time — convention over WebSocket configuration.
bytes.code_snippet:
# app/models/post.rb class Post < ApplicationRecord broadcasts_to ->(post) { :posts } end # app/views/posts/index.html.erb <%= turbo_stream_from :posts %>
<%= render @posts %>
# app/views/posts/_post.html.erb

<%= post.title %>

# From a background job — push anywhere: Turbo::StreamsChannel.broadcast_replace_to( :posts, target: dom_id(post), partial: "posts/post", locals: { post: post } )
